text: We now prepare to do a right pneumonectomy.
We begin with by reflecting the right lung laterally and doing so we can identify the hilus of the lung.
We identify the upper border and the lower border and when we incise the hilus we need to take care not to damage the phrenic nerve which we identify here.
running anterior to the hilus.
It runs between the mediastinal pleura and the fibrous pericardium.
We must take care not to damage it because it's the motor supply to the right hemidiaphragm.
This is a higher magnification view of the region of the hilus of the right lung.
For orientation here we again show the
the right lung here and the mediastinum has been pulled to the left and we have dissected free and isolated the phrenic nerve which we now identify.
We can see in the field here the superior vena cava running from superior to inferior to drain into the right atrium.
If we examine the region of the hilus, we can see the right main stem bronchus.
We'll see more of it shortly after we have divided the pulmonary artery, which was immediately anterior and inferior.
And then continuing inferiorly, we see the pulmonary veins.
We see a superior pulmonary vein and an inferior pulmonary vein.
We're now going to divide the structures of the hilus.
We begin by dividing the inferior pulmonary vein, which we divide now, and the superior pulmonary vein, which we divide now, and the branches of the pulmonary artery, which we divide now.
As we reflect and clean these structures, we'll soon have a clearer view of the right main stem bronchus.
We now show the pulmonary arteries, which we have divided here.
And we now divide the right main stem bronchus proximal to its
branch point.
In order to complete our removal of the lung we need to divide the connective tissue structures associated with the posterior layer of the hilus.
We now place our finger behind the hilus of the lung and we divide the connective tissue.
In doing so we're now able to remove the lung.
We've done some further dissection superior to the hilus of the lung in order to identify the vagus nerve.
We see here the vagus nerve located adjacent and just lateral to the trachea, which we indicate, and the esophagus, which is difficult to see posterior.

pulmonary branches.

the left pulmonary artery and inferiorly the superior pulmonary vein and the inferior pulmonary vein.

to the pulmonary and the cardiac plexus.
